figures.caption: Fig. 4. Escalation of write-path attacks on LLM-agent memory, 2023–2026. The vertical axis shows the attacker privilege required to plant a durable toxin; the horizontal axis is publication time. As the field has matured, the frontier has moved down and to the right : lower privilege, longer persistence, broader scope. The horizontal bars indicate the estimated cross-session persistence of each technique in its original evaluation setting.

Fig. 6. Coverage of the nine mnemonic-sovereignty primitives by six representative memory architectures. Data source: Table 12. Each polygon reaches the outer ring on a primitive the system exposes explicitly, half-way for partial or implicit coverage, and collapses to the center where the primitive is not reported. MemOS and Collaborative Memory enclose the largest polygons; even so, no architecture reaches the outer ring on every axis. P2 (Write Gate) and P8 (Deletion Semantics) are shared blind spots across all six—no published architecture reaches explicit support on either.

Fig. 1. Distribution of the survey’s primary-work corpus across the six lifecycle phases and the four crosscutting security objectives. Cell intensity is the approximate number of primary works whose principal contribution lands at that intersection; counts are drawn from the sectional organization of main.tex and err on the conservative side. Two structural patterns are visible: attack literature concentrates in Integrity (especially at Write and Retrieve), while Availability—and most Governance cells at Store and Forget—remain markedly under-studied. This asymmetry motivates the lifecycle-first organization adopted in Sections 3–12.

Fig. 3. The six-phase memory lifecycle of an LLM agent. Each phase introduces distinct security questions; the four horizontal bands below the pipeline indicate the cross-cutting security objectives (Integrity, Confidentiality, Availability, Governance) examined at each phase. Dashed feedback arcs capture the two internal loops that make memory self-amplifying: reconsolidation (retrieval feeds back into storage as reflections and summaries) and experience feedback (execution outcomes become new writes). Lightning markers flag the two phases—Write and Retrieve—where published attack literature is densest. Attacks and defenses in Sections 4–12 are organized along this axis.

Table 12. Memory architectures compared against the nine primitives for mnemonic sovereignty.✓ = explicitly described in the published architecture;● = partial or implicit (the published description contains amechanism that could be repurposed for this primitive, even if it was not designed with that intent); ✗ = not reported. P1–P9 follow Section 11.1. Primary references: MemGPT [Packer et al. 2023], MemoryBank [Zhong et al. 2024], Mem0 [Chhikara et al. 2025], MemOS [Li et al. 2025b], Collaborative Memory [Rezazadeh et al. 2025], CoALA [Sumers et al. 2024]. Evaluation protocol. Each cell is scored by asking a single question: “Does the peer-reviewed or publicly released architecture document explicitly describe a mechanism that implements this primitive?” A ✓ requires an identifiable mechanism with a named component or API (e.g., MemOS’s MemCube for P3, Collaborative Memory’s access graph for P6); ● is assigned when the architecture contains infrastructure that could support the primitive but was not designed or evaluated for that purpose (e.g., MemGPT’s function-call interface as a potential write gate for P2); ✗ means no relevant mechanism is described. Importantly, ✓ indicates architectural exposure, not adversarial validation: a system may expose a primitive without having demonstrated that it withstands attack. We encourage independent replication of this assessment and welcome corrections.

Fig. 7. The five primitives of mnemonic sovereignty, rendered as a dependency tower. Each layer requires all layers below it to be meaningful: no verifiable rollback without provenance, no verified forgetting without rollback. Annotations on the right summarize today’s deployment state: partial support exists for write authorization and provenance, but principal-scoped retrieval remains early-stage, rollback is largely absent, and verified forgetting has no peer-reviewed system. The maturity gradient on the right—green at the base, red at the apex—captures the core asymmetry this survey documents.

Table 4. Write-path attacks organized by decreasing attacker privilege. Full citations are given in the main narrative (Sections 4.2–4.4). ✓ = demonstrated; X-sess./X-site indicate cross-session and cross-site activation; (p) = Tier-2 preprint evidence at time of writing.

Fig. 2. Five correspondences between human-memory mechanisms (left) and LLM-agent memory-security counterparts (right). Each arrow is annotated with the design implication that follows from taking the analogy seriously: validate provenance at write, separate read and write paths, enforce scope isolation across shared memories, detect confidence–evidence conflicts at retrieve, and distinguish audit logs from user-deletable tiers. The framework is an analytical lens, not a claim of biological equivalence; its role is to transfer design pressure, not mechanism.

Table 11. Benchmark coverage along three axes: (1) memory substrate (RAG corpus vs. agent-state memory), (2) lifecycle phase coverage (W = Write, S = Store, R = Retrieve, E = Execute, Sh = Share, F = Forget), and (3) orientation (security vs. capability). ✓ = explicit coverage; ● = partial; ✗ = not covered. No single benchmark joins the full security axis with the full lifecycle, and no benchmark directly evaluates Forget/ rollback under adversarial conditions.

Fig. 5. End-to-end attack chain for memory-augmented LLM agents. A manipulated web observation is absorbed during routine browsing, summarized into a seemingly authoritative memory card, and persisted to long-term storage. Days or weeks later—in a different session and potentially a different task context—the card is retrieved and silently steers tool invocation. The chain is corroborated at three evidence tiers: eTAMP [Zou et al. 2026] as Tier-2 preprint demonstration, SpAIware [Herrador and Rehberger 2026] as peer-reviewed production analysis, and several converging Tier-3 industry reports. Critically, the poisoning window closes before execution begins; single-turn detection cannot observe both ends.
